title: Utah State vs Villanova — First Round Viewing Guide
eventTag: NCAA Tournament • First Round • San Diego, CA
venue: Viejas Arena, San Diego, California • TNT
extraInfo: Winner faces #1 Arizona or #16 LIU on Sunday

stakes.callout: A Mountain West double champion playing its final conference season before moving to the Pac-12 against a two-time national champion returning to March after a three-year absence. Utah State has the 20th-best offense in America and the MW Player of the Year. Villanova has a freshman All-Big East point guard, a 6-10 transfer averaging a double-double, and the ghost of Kris Jenkins's buzzer-beater. The 8-9 game is the ultimate coin flip — and the winner likely faces #1 Arizona.

cards.label: For Utah State
cards.value: 28-6 with the MW regular season AND tournament titles — just the seventh team in MW history to sweep both. Mason Falslev is the MW Player of the Year. This is their fourth straight tournament (school record) and their last season in the Mountain West before joining the Pac-12. They want to go out on top.

cards.label: For Villanova
cards.value: Back in the tournament for the first time since 2023 after a three-year drought. Kevin Willard's 24 wins are the most by a first-year Villanova coach ever. Acaden Lewis won Big East Freshman of the Year. Duke Brennan has 14 double-doubles. This is the beginning of Villanova's post-Jay Wright renaissance.

cards.label: The Coin Flip
cards.value: The 8-9 matchup is statistically the closest game in the tournament. Since 1985, 9-seeds have won 53 of 164 games against 8-seeds (32.3%). Utah State's offensive efficiency (20th) is dramatically better than Villanova's (76th). But Villanova has Big East battle scars and championship DNA.

cards.label: The Arizona Shadow
cards.value: The winner of this game almost certainly faces #1 Arizona on Sunday. Arizona is 32-2 with three potential lottery picks. Getting past the 8-9 game just earns you a date with one of the best teams in the country. How much gas will the winner have left?

styles.title: Mountain West Champions on a Mission
bullets: Top-20 offense: Utah State ranks 20th nationally in KenPom offensive efficiency (120.0) and averages 82.5 PPG. They shoot 49.1% from the field. This is a legitimately elite offense — better than Villanova's by a wide margin.

Falslev is the MW Player of the Year: Mason Falslev averages 16.1 PPG, 5.8 RPG, 3.1 APG on 41.1% from three. A Cache Valley native who does everything — scores, rebounds, passes, steals. The heart of this team.
Collins provides the scoring punch: MJ Collins (17.6 PPG) was the MW Tournament MVP after scoring 20 in the championship. He and Falslev form one of the best guard tandems outside power conferences.

Transition offense: Utah State thrives in the open court. They force 8.8 steals per game and convert turnovers into fast-break points. Their up-tempo style could overwhelm Villanova if the Wildcats can't match the pace.

Weakness: Free throw shooting is inconsistent — several players shoot below 70%. Defensive efficiency (70th) is solid but not elite. And the MW schedule didn't include the kind of opponents Villanova faced in the Big East.

styles.title: Championship Pedigree, New Foundation
bullets: Brennan's double-doubles: Duke Brennan (6-10, transfer from Grand Canyon) averages 12.4 PPG, 10.3 RPG with 14 double-doubles. He shoots 65.7% from the field. His physical interior presence gives Villanova an identity that Utah State can't easily match.

Lewis is the future: Freshman Acaden Lewis (12.3 PPG, 5.3 APG) won Big East Freshman of the Year and First-Team All-Big East. He runs the offense with poise beyond his years. A future star who's already producing at a high level.

Big East battle-testing: Villanova went 15-5 in the Big East, beating UConn and Marquette during the regular season. That conference gauntlet is tougher than anything Utah State faced in the Mountain West.
Askew stretches the floor: Devin Askew shoots 40.8% from three and provides the perimeter shooting Villanova needs to complement Brennan's interior game.

Weakness: Offensive efficiency (76th) and defensive efficiency (140th) are modest for an 8-seed. Free throw shooting (69.4%) is poor. Lost early in the Big East Tournament. No dominant go-to scorer when the game slows down.

keys.title: Utah State's offense vs. Villanova's interior.
keys.description: Utah State's offense ranks 20th in KenPom. Villanova's defense ranks 140th. That gap is enormous. But Villanova has Duke Brennan (6-10, 10.3 RPG) protecting the paint. If Utah State can score in transition and on the perimeter (Falslev shoots 41.1% from three), they exploit Villanova's defensive weakness. If Villanova slows the game and makes Utah State work in the half-court, Brennan's presence matters more.

keys.title: Brennan vs. Utah State's frontcourt.
keys.description: Duke Brennan averages a double-double at 65.7% from the field. Utah State's tallest regular is Karson Templin at 6-8. If Brennan dominates the paint, Villanova controls the game. If Utah State can force him into foul trouble or neutralize him with quickness, the Aggies' offensive firepower takes over.

keys.title: The freshman factor.
keys.description: Both teams rely on young players in critical roles. Villanova's Acaden Lewis (freshman, 5.3 APG) runs the offense. Utah State's Adlan Elamin (freshman, 6.5 PPG) and Elijah Perryman (freshman, 4.6 PPG) provide depth. Tournament pressure affects freshmen differently — some thrive, some freeze. Lewis's poise will be tested in his first March Madness game.

keys.title: Free throw shooting in crunch time.
keys.description: Both teams have free throw problems. Villanova shoots 69.4% as a team. Utah State has several players below 70%. In a close 8-9 game that could come down to the final minutes, whoever makes their free throws wins. This is the kind of fundamental that decides coin-flip games.

keys.title: The Arizona question.
keys.description: The winner almost certainly faces #1 Arizona on Sunday. Arizona is 32-2 with the best team in the West Region. Does either team subconsciously look ahead? Or does the 8-9 game serve as its own season — win or go home? Teams that play like they have nothing to lose tend to perform better in these matchups.

prediction.summary: The analytics clearly favor Utah State — their offense ranks 20th in KenPom compared to Villanova's 76th, and their guard tandem of Falslev and Collins is one of the best outside power conferences. But Villanova has Duke Brennan, a 6-10 double-double machine who gives them a physical identity Utah State can't easily match. The Aggies' tallest regular is 6-8. This game comes down to whether Utah State can shoot over and around Brennan's interior presence, or whether Villanova can slow the game enough to let Brennan's physicality control the pace. Both teams have free throw problems, which could make the ending ugly.

phases.title: Opening 10 Minutes
phases.description: Utah State comes out pushing tempo — they thrive in transition with 8.8 steals per game. Collins and Falslev attack early, and the Aggies' ball movement creates open threes. But Brennan is a problem immediately — he grabs three offensive rebounds in the first five minutes and converts two putbacks. It's 14-12 Utah State in a fast-paced start.

phases.title: Mid-First Half
phases.description: Villanova settles into their half-court offense. Lewis (5.3 APG) finds Brennan on the block repeatedly, and Askew stretches the defense with his 40.8% three-point shooting. Utah State's defense (70th KenPom) holds but doesn't dominate. Falslev answers with a pull-up three — he shoots 41.1% — and the teams trade baskets. Halftime: tied in the low 30s.

phases.title: Second Half
phases.description: Collins takes over. The MW Tournament MVP starts attacking the basket and getting to the line (82.4% FT shooter). Falslev adds a couple of assists and steals. Utah State pushes the lead to 6. But Lewis — the freshman Big East POY — shows composure beyond his years, orchestrating a 7-0 run with a three and two assists. Perkins hits a contested jumper. The game tightens.

phases.title: Final 5 Minutes
phases.description: Free throw shooting decides this coin-flip game. Both teams are shaky from the line, and a couple of critical misses swing momentum back and forth. Brennan picks up his fourth foul and has to sit for two minutes — Utah State attacks the paint in his absence. But he returns for the final 90 seconds and grabs a critical rebound. Collins hits a go-ahead floater. Utah State's transition defense gets a stop, and they advance.

projectedScore.rationale: Utah State's superior offensive efficiency and the Falslev-Collins backcourt are the difference. Brennan keeps Villanova close, but the Aggies' pace and shooting win out in a tight finish.

players.name: Mason Falslev
players.meta: G • 6-3 • Junior • Mountain West Player of the Year
players.bio:
A Cache Valley native who became the Mountain West Player of the Year. Leads the team in rebounds, minutes, and steals while scoring 16.1 PPG on 41.1% three-point shooting. Had 16 points, 6 assists, and 4 rebounds in the MW championship victory over San Diego State. The face of Utah State basketball.
Read more →

players.name: MJ Collins
players.meta: G • 6-4 • Senior • Mountain West Tournament MVP
players.bio: Senior who leads the team in scoring and was named MW Tournament MVP after scoring 20 points in the championship game. Shoots 49.1% from the field and 82.4% from the free throw line. The go-to scorer when Utah State needs a bucket in big moments.

players.name: Drake Allen
players.bio: Graduate guard who leads the team with 4.7 assists per game. Named to the MW All-Tournament team. The veteran facilitator who keeps the offense flowing and makes sure Falslev and Collins get their looks.

players.name: Karson Templin
players.bio: Junior forward who provides floor spacing at 35.1% from three while contributing 4.2 RPG. Utah State's best inside-outside blend — he'll need to match up against Brennan's physicality despite giving up two inches.

players.name: Duke Brennan
players.bio:
Transfer from Grand Canyon who averages a double-double with 14 double-doubles this season. Shoots 65.7% from the field. Had 20 points and 13 rebounds on Senior Day against Xavier. Willard recruited him specifically to give Villanova a physical interior identity. The most dominant rebounder in this game.
Read more →

players.name: Acaden Lewis
players.meta: G • 6-2 • Freshman • Big East Freshman of the Year, First-Team All-Big East
players.bio: True freshman who won Big East Freshman of the Year and First-Team All-Big East — an extraordinary double for a first-year player. Leads the team with 5.3 APG and runs the offense with composure that belies his age. This is his first tournament game. If he plays like a veteran, Villanova wins.

players.name: Tyler Perkins
players.bio: Junior guard who leads Villanova in scoring at 13.7 PPG. Named to the All-Big East team. A versatile wing who can score from all three levels and contributes 5.4 RPG.

players.name: Devin Askew
players.bio: Senior who shoots 40.8% from three — Villanova's most reliable perimeter weapon. A journeyman who found his role as a floor-spacing shooter in Willard's system. His shooting is critical to stretching defenses away from Brennan inside.

funFacts.title: Two National Championships (Villanova)
funFacts.body: Villanova won national titles in 2016 and 2018 under Jay Wright. The 2016 championship ended with Kris Jenkins's buzzer-beating three to beat North Carolina 77-74 — one of the most iconic moments in sports history.

funFacts.title: Last Dance in the Mountain West (Utah State)
funFacts.body: This is Utah State's final season in the Mountain West before joining the Pac-12 on July 1, 2026. They're leaving on top — winning both the regular season and tournament championships. Only the seventh team in MW history to sweep both.

funFacts.title: Cache Valley Native (Utah State)
funFacts.body: Mason Falslev is a Cache Valley native — meaning he grew up in the same valley where Utah State is located (Logan, Utah). He became the Mountain West Player of the Year in his own backyard.

funFacts.title: Bob Huggins Connection (Utah State)
funFacts.body: Coach Jerrod Calhoun was a student assistant under Bob Huggins at Cincinnati, then rejoined Huggins at West Virginia as director of basketball operations. The Huggins coaching tree now reaches to the Mountain West.

funFacts.title: The Augustinian Tradition (Villanova)
funFacts.body: Villanova was founded in 1842 by the Order of Saint Augustine. It's one of the oldest Catholic universities in the country. The campus is in suburban Philadelphia — just 12 miles from center city.

funFacts.title: From Grand Canyon to the Big East (Villanova)
funFacts.body: Duke Brennan transferred from Grand Canyon — a WAC school in Phoenix — to Villanova. He now averages a double-double (12.4/10.3) with 14 double-doubles in the Big East. That's a massive jump in competition.

funFacts.title: 193-13 at Home (Utah State)
funFacts.body: Under legendary coach Stew Morrill (1999-2015), Utah State went 193-13 at the Dee Glen Smith Spectrum — one of the best home records in college basketball history. The current team carries that home-court toughness on the road.

funFacts.title: Famous Wildcats (Villanova)
funFacts.body: Famous Villanova alumni include Jalen Brunson (NBA All-Star with the Knicks), former Pennsylvania Governor Ed Rendell, and actress Maria Bello.

funFacts.title: Four Straight Tournaments (Utah State)
funFacts.body: Utah State is making its fourth straight NCAA Tournament appearance — a school record. They've been in the tournament six of the last seven years. The program has been remarkably consistent through two coaching changes.

funFacts.title: Freshman Floor General (Villanova)
funFacts.body: Acaden Lewis won both Big East Freshman of the Year and First-Team All-Big East — one of the most decorated freshman seasons in recent conference history. He averages 5.3 APG and runs the offense.

funFacts.title: NAIA to D-I Success (Utah State)
funFacts.body: Coach Calhoun started his career on a NAIA national championship staff at Walsh University (2005). Then DII runner-up at Fairmont State. Then DI success at Youngstown State. Now MW champions at Utah State. A classic coaching climb.

funFacts.title: Three-Year Drought Over (Villanova)
funFacts.body: Villanova hadn't been to the NCAA Tournament since 2023. After Jay Wright retired and his successor went 32-37, Kevin Willard was hired and got them back in Year 1 with a program-record 24 wins for a first-year coach.

history.callout: Villanova has three national championships (1985, 2016, 2018) and 42 NCAA Tournament appearances. Utah State has 26 tournament appearances and a history of being one of the best mid-major programs in the country. This 8-9 matchup is a coin flip between a blue blood trying to reclaim its identity and a conference champion making its farewell tour.

timeline.text: Kris Jenkins hits a buzzer-beating three to beat North Carolina 77-74 for the national championship. One of the most iconic moments in sports.
Villanova wins its second championship in three years, beating Michigan 79-62. Jalen Brunson is the National Player of the Year.
Jay Wright retires abruptly. Kyle Neptune hired as successor.
Neptune goes 32-37 in two seasons. No tournament appearances. Three-year drought.
Kevin Willard hired from Maryland. Rebuilds the roster with transfers (Brennan from Grand Canyon) and recruits (Lewis).
24-8. First-Team All-Big East freshman. Back in the tournament. Willard's record 24 first-year wins.

subsections.title: Utah State's Mountain West Farewell
subsections.content:
Utah State has been a Mountain West member since 2013 and has been one of the conference's most consistent programs. Under Stew Morrill (1999-2015), the Aggies went to eight NCAA Tournaments and compiled a 193-13 home record. Under subsequent coaches, they've maintained the standard — this is their fourth straight tournament.

This season is special: it's Utah State's final year in the Mountain West before joining the Pac-12 in July 2026. Winning both the regular season and tournament championships is the perfect farewell. Jerrod Calhoun, in just his second year, has USU at 28-6 — one of the best seasons in program history.

A tournament win against Villanova would cap the Mountain West era with an exclamation point before the program enters a new chapter in a power conference.

subsections.title: The 8-9 Game: March's Coin Flip
subsections.content:
The 8-vs-9 matchup is the most evenly matched game in the tournament bracket. Since 1985, 8-seeds have won about 51% of the time — essentially a coin flip. Neither team has a meaningful seeding advantage.

What separates this particular 8-9? Utah State has the better offense by far (20th vs. 76th in KenPom). Villanova has the Big East pedigree and Brennan's interior dominance. The analytics slightly favor Utah State. The name on the jersey slightly favors Villanova. The winner faces #1 Arizona on Sunday — meaning this might be both teams' last game regardless.
